text: In this video, we describe a robotic heller myotomy and dorphin duplication performed for achalasia. Patient is a fifty nine year old male with a seven year history of dysphagia to solids and liquids. Esophageal manometry consistent with achalasia showed incomplete relaxation of the lower esophageal sphincter and a peristalsis. He has had a history of prior dilation and had an inability to tolerate an oral diet prior to surgery. Patient's barium esophagram shows a dilated distal esophagus without significant passage of contrast into the proximal stomach.
The surgical setup includes two 8mm robotic instrument ports and a 12mm midline port for the camera. The bedside assistant utilizes two 5mm ports.

The surgeon begins dissection at the pars flaccida using a harmonic scalpel, progressing towards the esophageal hiatus. The esophageal fat pad and esophagus are dissected from the right and left crura, enabling circumferential dissection of the distal esophagus. A pin rose is placed around the esophagus for traction, enhancing visualization of the esophageal hiatus for repair. A myotomy is performed on the distal esophagus, extending to the esophageal hiatus and three centimeters onto the stomach. The procedure concludes with an air test to check for mucosal perforation.
Following the completion of the myotomy, the surgeon repairs the esophageal hiatus by placing a single suture at the distal end to approximate the left and right crura.

The surgeon performs a Dor fundoplication, using a single suture to attach the proximal fundus to the left crura. The intracavitary articulation of the instrument facilitates easy suturing. A portion of the greater curvature of the stomach is sutured to the right crura, with the assistant reducing tension by bringing the stomach towards the hiatus. Two additional sutures are placed towards the hiatus to complete the fundoplication. The esophagogastric junction is checked for undue angulation after the procedure.
